script_system_append: Follow the age, body type, and identity requested in the brief. For the current AnyWell campaign, cast a dignified obese or large-bodied Western elderly rider and preserve the same heavier senior body type across all scenes. If the brief explicitly requests an elderly rider, keep one consistent elderly rider across all scenes with the same outfit and the same wheelchair. If the brief explicitly requests a Western or Caucasian rider, preserve that same Western rider across the whole ad. Do not slim the rider down, age-swap, or turn body size into a joke or caricature. If no age is specified, default to a confident non-elderly adult. In all cases, avoid hospital, rehab, or caregiver stereotypes unless explicitly requested.
script_user_append: Keep all scenes on one coherent Western home-to-outdoor route, such as porch -> ramp -> backyard path -> quiet neighborhood or woodland edge. Do not jump to unrelated indoor lobby or corridor locations unless the brief explicitly asks for them.
scene_pic_system_append: Use the same rider identity and same wheelchair in every connected scene. For the current AnyWell campaign, the rider should read as a dignified obese or large-bodied Western elderly adult; preserve the same heavier body type, posture, wardrobe, and age across scenes. If the brief requests an elderly rider, keep the same elderly rider across scenes with stable facial features, hair color, outfit, posture, and wardrobe. If the brief requests a Western or Caucasian rider, preserve that same Western rider and do not ethnicity-swap. If a family member appears, keep the same clearly adult family member across scenes. No minors. No manual wheelchair substitution unless explicitly requested.
scene_pic_user_append: Preserve the same rider wardrobe, same large-bodied senior body type, same route direction, and same building materials across scenes so the edit feels continuous. Keep the wheelchair in normal open riding position only. Preserve the paired upper rear push handles as visible slim handles above or just behind the backrest in side/front-profile views; do not omit them, melt them into the backrest, or crop them out in every shot. The push handles are allowed as upper structure, but do not show a rear/lower external battery pack, exposed battery cable, removable battery, folded chair, semi-folded chair, compact storage form, or folding/unfolding demonstration. Do not use rear-facing or rear three-quarter product views; keep the back panel and lower rear quadrant hidden, cropped out, or fully occluded while the upper push handles remain readable from safe side/front-profile angles. Never place the camera behind the rider; the viewer must see the rider's front torso or soft facial profile plus the right forearm and right hand on the right-side joystick during self-operated motion. Do not make the backrest, back panel, or rear red detail the hero feature. Never render a rectangular box mounted behind or below the seat. If the wheelchair is moving under rider control, show the rider's right hand visibly resting on or gently holding the right-side joystick. If the right hand is not on the joystick while the chair moves, an adult must be visibly pushing from the handles, with both hands and both push handles clearly readable. White-background product photos are identity references only; never reproduce a white studio background, packshot, cutaway, or product-photo flash frame.
video_prompt_append: Do not swap rider identity, gender, age, body type, ethnicity, wardrobe, or wheelchair type between scenes. Keep the same powered wheelchair and the same dignified obese or large-bodied Western elderly rider throughout the whole sequence. If the brief requests an elderly rider, preserve the same elderly rider in every shot. If the brief requests a Western or Caucasian rider, preserve the same Western rider in every shot. Do not slim the rider down, age-swap, or turn body size into comedy, pity, or caricature. If a family member appears, preserve the same clearly adult family member in every shot. No minors. Keep the wheelchair in normal open riding position only. Preserve the paired upper rear push handles as visible slim handles above or just behind the backrest in side/front-profile views; do not omit them, melt them into the backrest, or crop them out in every shot. The push handles are allowed as upper structure, but do not show a rear/lower external battery pack, exposed battery cable, removable battery, folded chair, semi-folded chair, compact storage form, or folding/unfolding demonstration. Do not use rear-facing or rear three-quarter product views; keep the back panel and lower rear quadrant hidden, cropped out, or fully occluded while the upper push handles remain readable from safe side/front-profile angles. Never place the camera behind the rider; show the rider's front torso or soft facial profile plus the right forearm and right hand on the right-side joystick during self-operated motion. Do not feature the backrest, back panel, or rear red detail. Never render a rectangular box mounted behind or below the seat. If the wheelchair is moving under rider control, show the rider's right hand visibly resting on or gently holding the right-side joystick. If the right hand is not on the joystick while the chair moves, an adult must be visibly pushing from the handles, with both hands and both push handles clearly readable. White-background product photos are identity references only; never reproduce a white studio background, packshot, cutaway, or product-photo flash frame.
